en.extract:
The Cape Verde storm petrel (Hydrobates jabejabe) is an oceangoing bird found in the Atlantic Ocean, especially around the islands of Cape Verde. It was at one time considered to be a subspecies of the band-rumped storm petrel, but is now considered to be a separate species by the International Ornithological Congress and other authorities.
They breed much of year but most nest in the winter. They are mainly nocturnal.


Description
This bird is darker in plumage and the white rump is less conspicuous than Leach's storm petrel.


References


External links
"World Birds Taxonomic List: Genera and species with citations". Zoonomen.net. Retrieved 10 March 2022.

The '''Cape Verde storm petrel''' ('''''Hydrobates jabejabe''''') is an oceangoing bird found in the [[Atlantic Ocean]], especially around the [[island]]s of [[Cape Verde]]. It was at one time considered to be a [[subspecies]] of the [[band-rumped storm petrel]], but is now considered to be a separate species by the [[International Ornithologists' Union|International Ornithological Congress]] and other authorities.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.uk400clubonline.co.uk/index.php?s=Oceanodroma+jabejabe |title=UK400 Club online.co.uk |access-date=2010-05-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141103173534/http://www.uk400clubonline.co.uk/index.php?s=Oceanodroma+jabejabe |archive-date=2014-11-03 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.rnbws.org.uk/news.php|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090316131929/http://www.rnbws.org.uk/news.php|title=Royal Naval Birdwatching Society (Viewed May 6, 2010)|archive-date=16 March 2009|access-date=10 March 2022}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Petrels, albatrosses – IOC World Bird List|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/petrels/|website=Worldbirdnames.org|access-date=2021-07-29|language=en-US}}</ref>

They breed much of year but most nest in the winter.<ref>Robb, M., Mullarney, K., and Sound Approach. (2008). Petrels Night and Day: A Sound Approach Guide. The Sound Approach: Dorset, UK.</ref> They are mainly nocturnal.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Medrano |first1=Fernando |last2=Militão |first2=Teresa |last3=Gomes |first3=Ivandra |last4=Sardà-Serra |first4=Mariona |last5=de la Fuente |first5=Mònica |last6=Dinis |first6=Herculano A. |last7=González-Solís |first7=Jacob |date=2022-11-04 |title=Phenological divergence, population connectivity and ecological differentiation in two allochronic seabird populations |journal=Frontiers in Marine Science |volume=9 |doi=10.3389/fmars.2022.975716 |issn=2296-7745|doi-access=free |hdl=2445/218867 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

==Description==
This bird is darker in [[plumage]] and the white rump is less conspicuous than [[Leach's storm petrel]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.xs4all.nl/~jnvdlaan/Cape_Verde_2009.pdf|title=Report of a Birding Trip to The Cape Verde Islands from 20th March to 27th March 2009|website=Xs4all.nl|access-date=10 March 2022}}</ref>
